sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=26, col=14).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 8, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Column 35 shifts UP by 1 (cyclic). Column 23 has a more complex pattern - the block at row 32 (w) didn't change while others did. Need to understand the full mechanic.
- Goal model: Need to figure out what clicking each red cross does and what the target configuration is.
- Plan: Click the middle red cross to see which columns it affects, then compare.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 2 of about 38 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see a clear pattern:
- Clicking the TOP red cross (row 17) shifts colors in columns 23 and 35 (VERTICAL shifts)
- Clicking the MIDDLE red cross (row 26) shifts colors in row 26 (HORIZONTAL shift)
The middle red cross at row 26 shifted colors horizontally across that row. The blocks at row 26, columns 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 44 all changed.
Let me check if it's a shift LEFT by 1:
Before: w, b, g, w, S, p, w, S, g (at cols 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 44)
After:  b, g, w, S, p, w, S, g, w
Shift left by 1: b, g, w, S, p, w, S, g, w - YES! That matches!
So clicking the middle red cross shifts colors LEFT by 1 along row 26.
Similarly, clicking the top red cross should shift colors UP by 1 along columns 23 and 35.
Wait, but for column 23, I found it wasn't a simple shift. Let me re-examine.
Actually, the first click was on the top red cross which affected columns 23 and 35. But the second click was on the middle red cross which affected row 26. The block at (26, 23) changed from g -> w in the second action. So the state of column 23 was modified by BOTH actions.
Let me reconsider. After the first action (click top red cross), column 23 became:
b, S, p, g, b, w, w, b, Y, g (rows 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 41, 44)
Then the second action (click middle red cross) changed (26, 23) from g to w. So now column 23 is:
b, S, p, w, b, w, w, b, Y, g
So the first action's effect on column 23 was:
Before: S, p, g, b, w, w, b, Y, g, S
After:  b, S, p, g, b, w, w, b, Y, g
Let me check shift up by 1 again:
After[0] = b = Before[6]? No, Before[6] = b. But Before[1] = p.
Hmm, still doesn't match a simple shift.
Wait, maybe the top red cross shifts colors in a DIFFERENT pattern. Let me think about what columns are affected.
The top red cross is at row 17. The columns affected were 23 and 35. But what about the horizontal row 17?
Looking at the first action's changes:
(17, 23): S -> b
(17, 26): b -> p
(17, 29): p -> Y
(17, 32): Y -> p
(17, 35): p -> b
So row 17 also changed! I missed that. Let me re-examine the first action's changes more carefully.
From the first action:
Changed blocks (23):
(17, 23): S -> b
(17, 26): b -> p
(17, 29): p -> Y
(17, 32): Y -> p
(17, 35): p -> b
(20, 23): p -> S
(20, 35): b -> S
(23, 23): g -> p
(23, 35): S -> g
(26, 23): b -> g
(26, 35): g -> w
(29, 23): w -> b
(29, 35): w -> g
(32, 35): g -> p
(35, 23): b -> w
(35, 35): p -> w
(38, 23): Y -> b
(38, 35): w -> b
(41, 23): g -> Y
(41, 35): b -> g
(44, 23): S -> g
(44, 29): p -> S
(44, 35): g -> p
So the top red cross affects:
- Row 17: cols 23, 26, 29, 32, 35
- Column 23: rows 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 35, 38, 41, 44
- Column 35: rows 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 41, 44
- Row 44: cols 23, 29, 35
Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me think about it differently. Maybe clicking a red cross shifts colors along a specific path/loop that starts from that cross.
For the top red cross (row 17), the path might be:
1. Horizontal right from the cross along row 17
2. Vertical down along column 35
3. Horizontal left along row 44
4. Vertical up along column 23
Let me trace this loop:
Row 17 (left to right): S(23), b(26), p(29), Y(32), p(35)
Col 35 (top to bottom): p(17), b(20), S(23), g(26), w(29), g(32), p(35), w(38), b(41), g(44)
Row 44 (right to left): g(35), S(29), S(23) - wait, row 44 has S at col 23 and 26, p at 29 and 32, g at 35
Col 23 (bottom to top): S(44), g(41), Y(38), b(35), w(32), w(29), b(26), g(23), p(20), S(17)
So the full loop would be:
S(17,23), b(17,26), p(17,29), Y(17,32), p(17,35), b(20,35), S(23,35), g(26,35), w(29,35), g(32,35), p(35,35), w(38,35), b(41,35), g(44,35), p(44,32), S(44,29), S(44,26), S(44,23), g(41,23), Y(38,23), b(35,23), w(32,23), w(29,23), b(26,23), g(23,23), p(20,23)
That's 26 blocks in the loop. Let me check if the shift is consistent.
Before loop: S, b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, S, S, g, Y, b, w, w, b, g, p
After loop:  b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, S, g, g, Y, b, w, w, b, g, p, S
Hmm, let me check shift by 1:
After[0] = b = Before[1] ✓
After[1] = p = Before[2] ✓
After[2] = Y = Before[3] ✓
After[3] = p = Before[4] ✓
After[4] = b = Before[5] ✓
After[5] = S = Before[6] ✓
After[6] = g = Before[7] ✓
After[7] = w = Before[8] ✓
After[8] = g = Before[9] ✓
After[9] = p = Before[10] ✓
After[10] = w = Before[11] ✓
After[11] = b = Before[12] ✓
After[12] = g = Before[13] ✓
After[13] = p = Before[14] ✓
After[14] = S = Before[15] ✓
After[15] = S = Before[16] ✓
After[16] = g = Before[17]? No, Before[17] = S
Hmm, After[16] = g but Before[17] = S. Let me recheck.
Wait, I need to be more careful about the loop order. Let me trace it step by step.
The loop goes:
1. Row 17, left to right: (17,23), (17,26), (17,29), (17,32), (17,35)
2. Col 35, top to bottom (excluding row 17): (20,35), (23,35), (26,35), (29,35), (32,35), (35,35), (38,35), (41,35), (44,35)
3. Row 44, right to left (excluding col 35): (44,32), (44,29), (44,26), (44,23)
4. Col 23, bottom to top (excluding rows 17 and 44): (41,23), (38,23), (35,23), (32,23), (29,23), (26,23), (23,23), (20,23)
So the loop is:
(17,23), (17,26), (17,29), (17,32), (17,35), (20,35), (23,35), (26,35), (29,35), (32,35), (35,35), (38,35), (41,35), (44,35), (44,32), (44,29), (44,26), (44,23), (41,23), (38,23), (35,23), (32,23), (29,23), (26,23), (23,23), (20,23)
That's 26 blocks. Before colors:
S, b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, ?, S, g, Y, b, w, w, b, g, p
Wait, I need to get the colors for row 44. From the ascii:
Row 44: GccccccccccccSScSScppcppcggcccccccccccccccGGG
Let me count: G + 12 c's + SS + c + SS + c + pp + c + pp + c + gg
So at cols: 13-14=SS, 16-17=SS, 19-20=pp, 22-23=pp, 25-26=gg
Wait, that doesn't match the segmentation which said (44, 23): S. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the original segmentation data:
id=70 size=4 ~(44,23) - S
id=71 size=4 ~(44,26) - S
id=72 size=4 ~(44,29) - p
id=73 size=4 ~(44,32) - p
id=74 size=4 ~(44,35) - g
So row 44 blocks: S(23), S(26), p(29), p(32), g(35)
Let me redo the loop:
(17,23)=S, (17,26)=b, (17,29)=p, (17,32)=Y, (17,35)=p,
(20,35)=b, (23,35)=S, (26,35)=g, (29,35)=w, (32,35)=g, (35,35)=p, (38,35)=w, (41,35)=b, (44,35)=g,
(44,32)=p, (44,29)=S, (44,26)=S, (44,23)=S,
(41,23)=g, (38,23)=Y, (35,23)=b, (32,23)=w, (29,23)=w, (26,23)=b, (23,23)=g, (20,23)=p
Before: S, b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, S, S, g, Y, b, w, w, b, g, p
After:  b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, S, g, g, Y, b, w, w, b, g, p, S
Check shift by 1:
After[0]=b=Before[1] ✓
After[1]=p=Before[2] ✓
After[2]=Y=Before[3] ✓
After[3]=p=Before[4] ✓
After[4]=b=Before[5] ✓
After[5]=S=Before[6] ✓
After[6]=g=Before[7] ✓
After[7]=w=Before[8] ✓
After[8]=g=Before[9] ✓
After[9]=p=Before[10] ✓
After[10]=w=Before[11] ✓
After[11]=b=Before[12] ✓
After[12]=g=Before[13] ✓
After[13]=p=Before[14] ✓
After[14]=S=Before[15] ✓
After[15]=S=Before[16] ✓
After[16]=g=Before[17] ✓ (Before[17]=S... wait no)
Hmm, Before[16] = S (44,26) and Before[17] = S (44,23).
After[16] = g but Before[17] = S. That doesn't match.
Wait, let me recheck. After the first action, (44, 29): p -> S. So After at position 15 (44,29) = S.
And (44, 23): S -> g. So After at position 17 (44,23) = g.
Let me redo:
Position 14: (44,32) Before=p, After=S
Position 15: (44,29) Before=S, After=S
Position 16: (44,26) Before=S, After=g  <-- this is wrong for shift
Position 17: (44,23) Before=S, After=g
Hmm, (44,26) changed from S to g, but in a shift by 1, it should become Before[17]=S.
And (44,23) changed from S to g, but in a shift by 1, it should become Before[18]=g. ✓
So position 16 doesn't match. Let me check if there's a block at (44, 26) that I'm missing or if the loop order is different.
Actually, wait. Looking at the change list from the first action:
(44, 23): S -> g
(44, 29): p -> S
(44, 35): g -> p
There's no (44, 26) change! So (44, 26) didn't change, meaning it's NOT part of this loop.
Let me re-examine which blocks are in the loop. The blocks that changed in the first action:
Row 17: 23, 26, 29, 32, 35 (5 blocks)
Col 35: 20, 23, 26, 29, 32, 35, 38, 41, 44 (9 blocks, excluding row 17)
Row 44: 23, 29, 35 (3 blocks, excluding col 35 which is already counted)
Col 23: 20, 23, 26, 29, 35, 38, 41 (7 blocks, excluding rows 17 and 44)
Wait, but (32, 23) didn't change (w -> w). So it's not in the loop either.
Total changed: 5 + 9 + 3 + 7 = 24. But the change list has 23 entries. Let me recount.
From the change list:
Row 17: (17,23), (17,26), (17,29), (17,32), (17,35) = 5
Col 35: (20,35), (23,35), (26,35), (29,35), (32,35), (35,35), (38,35), (41,35), (44,35) = 9
Row 44: (44,23), (44,29) = 2 (excluding (44,35) already counted)
Col 23: (20,23), (23,23), (26,23), (29,23), (35,23), (38,23), (41,23) = 7 (excluding (17,23) and (44,23))
Total: 5 + 9 + 2 + 7 = 23 ✓
So the loop excludes (44,26) and (32,23). These are the "barrier" blocks.
Let me redo the loop without those:
(17,23), (17,26), (17,29), (17,32), (17,35),
(20,35), (23,35), (26,35), (29,35), (32,35), (35,35), (38,35), (41,35), (44,35),
(44,29), (44,23),
(41,23), (38,23), (35,23), (29,23), (26,23), (23,23), (20,23)
That's 5 + 9 + 2 + 7 = 23 blocks.
Before: S, b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, S, g, Y, b, w, b, g, p
After:  b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, S, g, Y, b, w, w, g, p, S
Check shift by 1:
After[0]=b=Before[1] ✓
After[1]=p=Before[2] ✓
...continuing...
After[22]=p=Before[23]? But there are only 23 elements (0-22).
After[22]=p=Before[23] would be Before[0]=S. That doesn't match.
After[22]=p should = Before[0]=S for a cyclic shift. But After[22]=p ≠ S.
Hmm, let me recheck. After[22] is the last element in the loop, which is (20,23).
Before (20,23) = p
After (20,23) = S (from change list: (20,23): p -> S)
So After[22] = S = Before[0] = S ✓
OK so I had the wrong value. Let me redo:
After: b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, S, g, Y, b, w, w, g, S
Check:
After[20]=w, Before[21]=w ✓
After[21]=g, Before[22]=p ✗
Hmm, After[21] should be Before[22]=p but it's g. Let me recheck.
Position 21 is (26,23). Before (26,23) = b, After (26,23) = g.
Position 22 is (23,23). Before (23,23) = g, After (23,23) = p.
Position 23 would be (20,23). Before (20,23) = p, After (20,23) = S.
Wait, I had 23 elements. Let me recount:
0: (17,23)
1: (17,26)
2: (17,29)
3: (17,32)
4: (17,35)
5: (20,35)
6: (23,35)
7: (26,35)
8: (29,35)
9: (32,35)
10: (35,35)
11: (38,35)
12: (41,35)
13: (44,35)
14: (44,29)
15: (44,23)
16: (41,23)
17: (38,23)
18: (35,23)
19: (29,23)
20: (26,23)
21: (23,23)
22: (20,23)
That's 23 elements. Before: S, b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, g, Y, b, w, b, g, p
After:  b, p, Y, p, b, S, g, w, g, p, w, b, g, p, S, g, Y, b, w, b, g, p, S
Check shift by 1:
After[20]=g, Before[21]=g ✓
After[21]=p, Before[22]=p ✓
After[22]=S, Before[0]=S ✓
So it IS a shift by 1! The loop has 23 elements and clicking the top red cross shifts colors along this loop by 1 position.
Now I understand the mechanic: each red cross controls a loop of blocks, and clicking it shifts colors along that loop.
For the middle red cross (row 26), the loop is horizontal along row 26:
(26,17), (26,20), (26,23), (26,26), (26,29), (26,32), (26,35), (26,38), (26,44)
That's 9 blocks. Before: w, b, g, w, S, p, w, S, g
After: b, g, w, S, p, w, S, g, w
Shift left by 1: b, g, w, S, p, w, S, g, w ✓
So the middle red cross shifts colors LEFT by 1 along row 26.
Now I need to figure out:
1. What loop does the bottom red cross control?
2. What is the target configuration (goal state)?
Let me think about the goal. In Level 1, the goal was to make all blocks the same color. Here, maybe the goal is to match the red cross color to the green cross color, or to create a path of matching colors.
Actually, looking at the layout again, each lane has a red cross on the left and a green cross on the right. The colored blocks form paths between them. Maybe the goal is to make the path from red to green all the same color, or to match specific colors.
Let me try clicking the bottom red cross to understand its loop, and also look for patterns that indicate the goal.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking a red cross shifts colors along a loop of blocks. Top red cross shifts a rectangular loop (23 blocks) clockwise. Middle red cross shifts row 26 left by 1 (9 blocks). Each red cross controls a different loop.
Goal model: Need to understand the target configuration - possibly matching colors along paths from red to green crosses.
Plan: Click the bottom red cross to understand its loop, then figure out the goal.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Save current state
current_blocks = {}
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        top = min(p[0] for p in b)
        left = min(p[1] for p in b)
        current_blocks[(top, left)] = n['color']

# Click bottom red cross at ~(35, 14)
predict(expect_change=True, note='Clicking bottom red cross')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 14}])

# Compare
new_blocks = {}
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        top = min(p[0] for p in b)
        left = min(p[1] for p in b)
        new_blocks[(top, left)] = n['color']

changed = []
for pos in sorted(set(list(current_blocks.keys()) + list(new_blocks.keys()))):
    old = current_blocks.get(pos, '?')
    new = new_blocks.get(pos, '?')
    if old != new:
        changed.append((pos, old, new))

print(f"Changed blocks ({len(changed)}):")
for pos, old, new in changed:
    print(f"  {pos}: {old} -> {new}")

# Group by row and col to see the loop
by_row = defaultdict(list)
by_col = defaultdict(list)
for pos, old, new in changed:
    by_row[pos[0]].append((pos[1], old, new))
    by_col[pos[1]].append((pos[0], old, new))

print("\nBy row:")
for row in sorted(by_row.keys()):
    print(f"  row {row}: {by_row[row]}")
print("\nBy col:")
for col in sorted(by_col.keys()):
    print(f"  col {col}: {by_col[col]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  (35, 38): b -> g
  (35, 41): g -> S
  (35, 44): S -> w

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking a red cross shifts colors along a loop of blocks. Top red cross shifts a rectangular loop (23 blocks) clockwise. Middle red cross shifts row 26 left by 1 (9 blocks). Each red cross controls a different loop.
Goal model: Need to understand the target configuration - possibly matching colors along paths from red to green crosses.
Plan: Click the bottom red cross to understand its loop, then figure out the goal.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Save current state
current_blocks = {}
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        top = min(p[0] for p in b)
        left = min(p[1] for p in b)
        current_blocks[(top, left)] = n['color']

# Click bottom red cross at ~(35, 14)
predict(expect_change=True, note='Clicking bottom red cross')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 14}])

# Compare
new_blocks = {}
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        top = min(p[0] for p in b)
        left = min(p[1] for p in b)
        new_blocks[(top, left)] = n['color']

changed = []
for pos in sorted(set(list(current_blocks.keys()) + list(new_blocks.keys()))):
    old = current_blocks.get(pos, '?')
    new = new_blocks.get(pos, '?')
    if old != new:
        changed.append((pos, old, new))

print(f"Changed blocks ({len(changed)}):")
for pos, old, new in changed:
    print(f"  {pos}: {old} -> {new}")

# Group by row and col to see the loop
by_row = defaultdict(list)
by_col = defaultdict(list)
for pos, old, new in changed:
    by_row[pos[0]].append((pos[1], old, new))
    by_col[pos[1]].append((pos[0], old, new))

print("\nBy row:")
for row in sorted(by_row.keys()):
    print(f"  row {row}: {by_row[row]}")
print("\nBy col:")
for col in sorted(by_col.keys()):
    print(f"  col {col}: {by_col[col]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
